entry.title: Sapphire 2026. The future of UX is here. Are you ready for an AI -powered world?
entry.content:
Sapphire 2026 accelerated SAP’s pivot to AI. Most of the keynote demos were heavy on AI, including introducing new engagement layer experiences to complement the existing application experiences. The magic of the engagement layer is that it allows you to interact with your ERP system in a conversational way, starting with your intent and the system will generate an interface based on the context of your request. While this might represent a more natural way to work, it also represents a major change to how people have been working with SAP applications for the past few decades.

The new engagement layer lets you interact with SAP in a conversational way.

I had the opportunity to see many deep-dive sessions that explained the new UX and how we expect people to work with it. More importantly, I spoke with many customers and partners about their reaction to the way we are moving. Generally, people are excited about the direction we are going and having more automation to free up time for deeper thought and analysis, but they are concerned about trusting AI to do things that might have significant business impact. While Joule Work will certainly be a game changer for many, the big open question is how to get there. As with all IT transformations, it will involve a combination of changes across people, processes, and technology.

Embedded AI meets customers where they are

The first session that I presented was on “Creating better user experiences with AI-powered SAP Fiori apps ASUG.” I started out by talking about overcoming usability challenges with AI and then did a live demo of around 10 cool productivity features. What’s unique about the embedded AI capabilities in SAP Cloud ERP is that it is built into the existing interfaces that people know, so it allows them to go through their work more quickly by using AI to search, summarize, and enter data.

There are many AI-powered features that make people more productive when using SAP Cloud ERP.

The slides from the session (PUX1228) include links where you can see short recorded demos of these features. These capabilities are available for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. Some of them are coming to private cloud in upcoming releases.

Some questions that customers asked that might be of general interest:

  • Does the engagement layer replace SAP Fiori? No – the application experience and engagement layer experience are complementary. They will coexist for the foreseeable future. (Thanks for the question, Tony)
  • Does the engagement layer use SAP Fiori elements? The answer here is more nuanced. We use the OData metadata that is used by Fiori elements to get the SAP S/4HANA data to render high quality cards in the engagement layer.
    So, the engagement layer is independent of the Fiori apps themselves. The engagement layer does not call the Fiori apps for the data, but it uses the same underlying data source, which are the OData services and annotations. (Great question, Kelly)

Lots of people were interested in having a better user experience with AI assistance.

Fear, optimism, and the AI Skills gap

My other presentation was a “community conversation” in the ASUG Hub. My colleague, Nicole Smythe and I facilitated the session along with Liz Tuckwell and Nadalee Williams from ASUG. The goal was to collect ideas on AI adoption from the attendees and equip them with information so they could create a 90-day plan to turn their thoughts into action and move forward with AI in your organization. After providing an overview of Joule Work, we broke into teams and people discussed barriers to implementing AI in their organizations and how they overcame them.

Top problems that the audience identified included:

  • Data. How to get the data clean so any AI agents can work properly and consistently. Most people know they need to fix their business processes and clean their data before they can benefit from AI.
  • Governance. People see the need for IT to manage this centrally. Currently, many innovative teams want to get started with AI and are doing things on their own without corporate oversight. We saw this situation a decade ago when some business managers purchased cloud services for sales or HR or other domains if they did not receive sufficient support from their IT teams.
  • Cost. How to meter AI consumption and allocate charges to different users.
  • AI Expertise. Upskilling teams on AI and its capabilities. Currently, this is a side job for most people, so they are not putting enough focus on this. Once company had a rotation program where top developers could allocate 50% of their time to learning AI topics and then bring those skills back to their teams.
  • Talent management. With fewer entry-level workers or people doing repetitive jobs, it is harder to identify the best employees, so you can focus on retention and building a leadership pipeline.

Participants discussed how to overcome barriers to AI adoption.

Other interesting sessions on AI, UX, and AI+UX

Most of the other sessions I attended were on AI, UX, or their intersection. Hearing the questions people asked and what they talked about afterwards will be helpful in refining our agenda for our next SAP Connect Day for UX, which is in Palo Alto on June 11. We’ll go deep on our UX strategy and share some new functionality, both released and unreleased. There will be two hands-on exercises, one on building applications and the other building Smart Helpers (currently in beta).

  • Katja Zoch and Sebastian Steinhauer had a session on how to extend SAP S/4HANA Cloud while keeping a clean core. They focused on what is clean core, why its important, and how to extend your core ERP system using clean core principles.  
  • Chris Hoffmann had several sessions on the show floor. They all focused on different dimensions of the AI-powered user experience in the SAP Business Suite. One was a broad survey of functionality, another focused on AI-assisted task automation, and the other talked about roadmap items. All showed how AI adoption is on the path towards human‑supervised autonomous ERP.
  • Tom Reiss presented on SAP Fiori in action, highlighting how modern UX helps users get their work done more easily and efficiently. He also had a roadmap session with Christina Salwizek featuring web and mobile innovations that are on the horizon.
  • Sophia Levens had some great sessions on the new Joule user experience, focusing on both how it works and the implications for users. She also had a strategy-focused talk with SAP’s chief designer officer, Arin Bhowmick, where they explored where user engagement and enterprise UX are headed.
  • Bert Schultz and Esther Vrieze spoke about different ways to migrate to SAP S/4HANA and the relative benefits among the different approaches.
  • Maura Hameroff and Rudi Hois talked about SAP Cloud ERP Private and the many innovations that customers can expect to see in upcoming releases.

We held our SAP Fiori Development Roundtable live from the show

To add a little extra energy to our already lively discussions, I joined my co-host Sebastian Werner live from the show floor. We spent lots of time talking about Joule Studio and how it fits with our existing development tools. If you want to join the next monthly call, please register.

AI vibe from across the conference

While the sessions are a great way to learn about hot topics, the best way to hear about how customers are using our solutions and what they are planning is in the hallways, food areas, UXpresso Café (successor to the Fiori Café), happy hours, and demo pods. The questions after sessions are also revealing. This year, the customers I spoke to were concerned about:

  • How the new engagement layer (Joule Work) fits with SAP Fiori
  • How to get started with AI, especially in private cloud and on-prem environments
  • If their existing Fiori apps will continue to work (yes!) and if they should continue to build new Fiori apps (yes!)
  • All the topics listed above from the ASUG community conversation (data, governance, cost, AI skills, and talent pipeline)

Next steps

Perhaps you are still digesting all the announcements from Sapphire and figuring out their impact on your business. To help you prepare for the future, consider discussing the following questions with your team.

  1. What business problems are most pressing and can benefit from automation? How will we meter the costs, set appropriate limits, and measure the return on investment?
  2. Is our data clean, consistent across different parts of the business, ready to train the AI system, and be part of automated workflows? How much autonomy are we willing to give agents and what is the best way to supervise / manage / audit their activities?
  3. How will we integrate AI into our SAP landscape? Are we using SAP’s embedded capabilities, Joule, the new engagement layer, or external AI-powered apps running on SAP data (subject to the API policy)?
  4. How will we address change management with our business users?
  5. How will we manage AI governance, risk, and security? Using SAP’s AI simplifies the answer to this question as we cover much of this, but you still need robust AI policies in your organization

If we did not meet at Sapphire and you are interested in joining the conversation about SAP Fiori application development and how AI makes it better, faster, and cheaper, please join our monthly SAP Fiori development roundtable group or sign up for our bi-monthly newsletter.

If you are in the Silicon Valley area or can fly there on June 11, please join us for SAP Connect Day for UX. We'll go deeper into all the UX and related AI topics listed in this post.
https://events.sap.com/us-ux-innovation-day-siliconvalley-2026/en_us/home.html 

For the UX Foundation team, Peter Spielvogel.

entry.title: AI Product Management: How Do You Know the Agent Is Actually Working?
entry.content:
Post 5 — AI Product Management series

The demo worked. The QA lead pulled up the test matrix: forty-three test cases, each traced back to an acceptance criterion, each one marked green. The engineering manager said "we're good." You said "ship it."

You have done this a hundred times. Requirements become stories, stories become tests, tests become evidence, evidence becomes a release decision. The whole system runs on traceability.

Now your team is telling you the AI agent is ready to ship. They ran evals. Every test passed. The results report looks a lot like the QA matrix you just signed off on.

It doesn't mean the same thing. The mental model that has served you for twenty years breaks in exactly three places when the system stops executing code and starts making decisions. The checkmarks are still there. What they prove is not.

Break 1: Non-determinism

In traditional software QA, a passing test proves the code will behave correctly. Given the same input, the same code produces the same output every time. One green checkmark means the behavior is fixed.

In agentic AI, one green checkmark is a sample from a distribution. The same agent, given the same task on different days, may succeed, fail, or take a path you did not anticipate. This is not a bug. It is the fundamental behavior of a probabilistic system.

The practical consequence: binary pass/fail is not a sufficient quality gate for agentic AI. The correct measure is Pass@K: run the same evaluation K times, typically between eight and twenty for production evaluations, and report the distribution of pass rates, not the result of a single run.

A Pass@K report tells you two things that a single-run result cannot. The P50, or median pass rate, is the expected performance in a typical interaction. An 85 percent P50 means the agent succeeds in 85 out of 100 interactions on average. The P10 is the performance in the worst-case 10 percent of runs. A P10 of 40 percent means that in bad scenarios, the agent succeeds less than half the time. The P10 is the number that determines user experience at scale, and it is the number most teams never calculate.

One passing test does not prove fixed behavior. It proves the system worked once. The question to ask when your team presents eval results is not "did it pass?" It is "how many times did you run it, and what was the distribution?"

Break 2: Compound probability

Suppose your agentic system runs a ten-step workflow: search, retrieve, filter, extract, format, validate, draft, review, send, log. Every single step passes at 95 percent accuracy in isolation. In a traditional system, you sign off.

In an agentic system, 95 percent accuracy across ten steps produces an end-to-end success rate of roughly 60 percent. The math: 0.95 to the power of 10 equals 0.598. The system fails more than 40 percent of the time while every individual component appears to pass.

For a five-step workflow with 95 percent per-step accuracy: 77 percent end-to-end success. For a ten-step workflow with 99 percent per-step accuracy: 90 percent end-to-end success.

Enterprise deployment thresholds for mission-critical workflows are typically 95 to 99 percent end-to-end reliability. Achieving 99 percent end-to-end on a ten-step workflow requires 99.9 percent per-step accuracy, which is above the current performance of frontier models on complex judgment tasks.

Do the compound probability calculation before any multi-step agentic deployment. It is arithmetic. Multiply the per-step pass rates. If the result is below your acceptable end-to-end threshold, the workflow cannot be safely deployed at that level of autonomy. This calculation frequently reveals that a multi-step autonomous workflow is not deployable as an Autonomous Actor, but is deployable as a Copilot if human review is inserted at the steps with the lowest per-step accuracy.

Break 3: Silent background failure

Traditional software that fails stops working, or produces an error state that is visible and actionable. The CI/CD pipeline turns red. The error log fills up.

Agentic AI that fails keeps working. It produces outputs. The outputs are fluent and confident. They are also increasingly wrong. There is no error state because the system is not in an error state. It is in a degraded state.

This is not hypothetical. Audits of production deployments have found cases where "order updated and closed" messages never corresponded to any underlying API call. The agent wrote the right words, the automated judge scored the output as accurate, and the test passed. No state validation layer. Invisible to metrics until someone looked directly at the system state.

This distinction matters: semantic validation asks whether the agent said the right thing. State validation asks whether the target system actually changed. An agent that produces a flawless description of an action it failed to execute will score perfectly on semantic validation and zero on what actually matters. Every action the agent takes must have a corresponding state validation check, the specific system state that should result from this action and the automated verification that confirms the state change happened.

The judge problem

LLM-as-judge, using a second language model to evaluate the quality of the first model's outputs, is the standard approach to scaling evaluation. It works. It also introduces a specific risk.

If the judge model was trained on similar data to the agent model, both share the same gaps. The judge cannot see what the agent cannot see. High judge scores can mask correlated failures.

The metric that determines whether the judge is useful is True Negative Rate: the proportion of real failures that the judge correctly identifies. A judge with 95 percent overall accuracy and 40 percent TNR is catching fewer than half of the real failures while appearing to perform well. A judge with 80 percent overall accuracy and 90 percent TNR is catching nearly all real failures and providing meaningful coverage.

Before trusting any LLM-as-judge system, calibrate it against a set of human-labeled known failures and measure TNR directly. This step is skipped by most teams. It must be repeated any time either the agent model or the judge model is updated.

The production gap

Evaluation performance on benchmark test sets systematically overstates production performance. Research published in 2025 found an average 37 percent gap between benchmark scores and production environment performance across enterprise agentic deployments.

The benchmark environment is controlled and representative of how the team imagined the system would be used. Production is not. Real users do not follow the happy path. Real data is messier. Real edge cases have not been anticipated. A system with 90 percent benchmark performance should be expected to perform at roughly 57 percent in production. A benchmark score is a ceiling, not a floor. Build the gap assumption into your launch criteria.

Three launch deliverables

Pass@K report. K runs across happy path, edge cases, adversarial inputs, and any available distribution shift cases. Reported as a distribution, not a mean. The P10 score must exceed the defined minimum acceptable performance threshold. The PM signs off on this threshold before evaluation begins, not after results are in.

Judge calibration record. TNR measured against a human-labeled failure set of at least 50 cases. Minimum acceptable TNR defined and signed off before evaluation begins. Re-calibration date scheduled.

State validation spec. For every action the agent takes: the specific system state that should result and the automated check that confirms the state change happened. Not a description of expected behavior. A specification of how the system verifies the behavior occurred.

A case the industry learned from

In 2021, a University of Michigan research team published an external audit of the most widely deployed sepsis prediction model in American hospitals, embedded in Epic's electronic health record platform and running in hospitals across the United States for six to eight years.

The vendor's claimed performance was an AUC of 0.76 to 0.83. The external audit measured an AUC of 0.63. Sensitivity was 33 percent. Positive predictive value was 12 percent. The model identified one out of three real sepsis cases correctly.

The model did not fail loudly. It produced confident alerts. Clinical teams adapted their behavior around it. The performance gap persisted for six to eight years. No regulator measured it. The vendor had not validated it against external data. The hospital customers had not audited it.

Any enterprise agentic product that ships with benchmark-based launch criteria and no post-launch performance monitoring is susceptible to the same pattern. The system continues operating. Performance continues degrading. Adoption metrics look fine. No alarm fires.

Engineers build evaluation infrastructure. The PM owns what gets tested, what counts as a pass, and what risk tolerance the team is operating at. These are product decisions, not engineering decisions. A PM who cannot answer what the P10 score is, whether the judge's TNR was measured, and whether state validation exists for every action has not shipped an agentic feature. Someone else made those decisions for them.

The next post is: What You Need to Watch After You Ship

This post is part of the AI Product Management series on SAP Community. The series is based on "Agentic AI for Product Managers," a lifecycle framework for building, evaluating, and governing agentic systems.

entry.title: AI Product Management: What You Need to Watch After You Ship
entry.content:
Post 6 — AI Product Management series

The call came from a supplier. She had been receiving order notifications all week and wanted to confirm the delivery schedule. Someone on the procurement team pulled up the dashboard: 340 transactions, 100 percent completion, every entry marked confirmed.

She was still on the line.

Not one order had arrived. The agent had been confirming work it never did, for six months.

The completion metric was real. The work was not.

Launch is not the end

In traditional product management, launch is the culmination of the development cycle. You ship, you measure, you iterate. The post-launch work is about improving a product that is essentially doing what it is supposed to do.

For agentic AI, launch is the moment when the real questions begin. The evaluation was a hypothesis about how the system would behave. Production is the test. And unlike a deterministic system, whose production behavior is predictable from the spec, an agentic system's behavior can diverge from evaluation behavior in ways that are invisible until they become consequential.

The PM who treats launch as the end of a project and hands it off to operations has not shipped an agentic product. They have deployed an unmonitored system.

Why traditional monitoring fails

Standard product monitoring is designed for deterministic systems. It catches binary failures: the API returned a 500 error, the transaction did not complete, the feature did not load. These are conditions the system can report on itself.

An agentic system that is performing below expectations does not report a 500 error. It produces outputs. The outputs are coherent. They pass basic quality checks. They are less accurate than they were at launch, in ways that accumulate slowly enough that no threshold is ever crossed.

Utilization metrics go up. Completion rates look fine. Time-to-completion is acceptable. The dashboard shows green. Meanwhile, 70 percent of the agent's outputs are being significantly rewritten by users who do not trust them, but who have not been given a feedback mechanism to surface that distrust. Traditional monitoring cannot catch this.

Automation complacency: the predictable failure of supervision

When a system performs reliably over time, the people supervising it adapt. This is rational behavior. If the agent has been right 98 percent of the time for six months, spending thirty seconds carefully reviewing each output before approving it is not efficient supervision. It is expensive theater.

Supervisors learn to trust the system. They start approving faster. They stop reading as carefully. The supervision that appeared to be a meaningful check becomes a rubber stamp.

This is automation complacency: not a character flaw in the supervisors, but the predictable behavioral output of a supervisory role that was never designed to maintain vigilance as conditions change.

The dangerous moment arrives when system performance degrades. The supervisors are calibrated to the system's performance from several months ago. The system is performing worse. The supervisors do not know this, because nothing told them.

Tesla Autopilot provides the most extensively documented example outside healthcare. The NHTSA documented 467 crashes, 54 injuries, and 14 deaths attributable in part to drivers who had learned to trust the system enough to reduce their attentiveness, and then were not able to intervene when the system failed. "Keep your hands on the wheel" was stated clearly in the user agreement. It was not a sustainable design constraint. The system had to hold the safety requirement, because the instruction alone would not hold it over time. Enterprise agentic products are in the same position.

The six observation instruments

 

Six metrics, designed before launch, provide meaningful coverage of what traditional monitoring misses. They are not dashboards to build after you ship. They are design requirements that must be defined before the product ships, because several of them can only exist if the corresponding design artifact from the previous post was built correctly.

Task success rate. Did the agent do what the user actually intended, not just what it reported doing? Most teams track whether the agent completed the task. The correct version measures whether it completed the task the user intended. The procurement agent in this post's opening had a 100 percent completion rate and a 0 percent task success rate. Task success requires a definition of success that goes beyond "the agent completed without an error state."

Unintended action rate. Actions taken outside the authorized autonomy boundary. This metric can only exist if the autonomy boundary was designed to be logged. If your team cannot produce this metric, the autonomy boundary was not designed. It exists only as an implicit assumption.

Override frequency. The rate at which users modify, reject, or override the agent's outputs before acting on them. Too high suggests a trust problem. Too low in a high-uncertainty domain suggests supervision has stopped, not that the agent has become perfect. Override frequency trending over time is also the earliest signal of supervision erosion. If it is persistently declining in a domain with genuine uncertainty, one of three things is true: the outputs are uniformly excellent (test this directly), users have moved their real work to a parallel process, or users have stopped engaging critically. Three distinct product problems, three different responses.

Confidence calibration. The relationship between the agent's expressed certainty and its actual accuracy. A miscalibrated agent that is highly confident on cases it gets wrong builds fragile trust: trust that holds until the first visible failure, then collapses faster than it was built. Confidence legibility is a design surface. Most teams are not designing it.

Rollback time. The time from identifying an incorrect action to completing a correcting action. This metric exists as a real number only if the recovery workflow was built as a product surface. If rollback time is measured by how long an email thread took to resolve the problem, the recovery workflow does not exist as a designed artifact.

Incident recovery time. When the agent misbehaves at scale: the time from first signal of a problem to freezing new invocations, attributing the cause, notifying affected users, and restoring safe operation. If that sequence was never designed before launch, incident recovery time measures the speed of improvisation.

The absence of a metric is a design gap, not a data gap

There is a forcing function in the measurement problem that exposes the design gap directly.

If a team cannot produce the override frequency metric, the approval moment was not designed to be legible or logged. If they cannot produce rollback time, recovery was not treated as a product surface. If they cannot produce a confidence calibration curve, confidence was not surfaced to the user in a measurable form.

The absence of the metric is not a data infrastructure problem. It confirms that the corresponding design artifact was never built.

This is the reversal that agentic products require. In traditional SaaS, you design the product and instrument it afterward. In agentic product design, the metric is the design requirement stated in advance. You design the approval moment because you need to measure override frequency. You design the recovery workflow because rollback time must become a sprint target. You design the audit surface because traceability is a product commitment, not a logging exercise.

Shadow workflows: what adoption numbers hide

Shadow workflows are the parallel manual processes that teams maintain alongside an AI feature when they do not trust the agent enough to rely on it for consequential decisions. The workflow runs. The metrics look good. The actual work is happening somewhere else.

A sales team using an AI feature that drafts account plans achieves 90 percent adoption within two months. The PM reports strong results at the quarterly review. Closer analysis finds that 70 percent of those plans are being significantly rewritten before submission. The representatives open the draft, extract the structure, discard most of the content, and write a new plan. The agent saved them from a blank page. It did not save them from the work.

The metric tracked was opens. The metric that would have revealed the quality problem was override frequency, which was never instrumented.

Deployment events as governance triggers

The observation instruments must be re-run whenever any component the agent depends on changes. Three categories of change constitute deployment events.

A model update from the provider. Any change to the foundation model version requires re-running the full evaluation suite and establishing a new behavioral baseline before restoring production traffic. Do not assume that a model update with no announced behavioral changes is behaviorally neutral.

A prompt change. Any change to the agent's instructions, including "minor clarifications" and "small wording improvements," alters behavior across the full input distribution. Treat every prompt change as a release requiring a new Pass@K run.

A tool configuration change. Any change to the tools the agent can access, including permission scope changes and API version updates, changes the agent's action surface. Requires boundary revalidation and a new audit baseline.

What the PM owns

Product lifecycle management is a PM responsibility. Not the launch. The loop.

Engineering builds the instrumentation. The PM defines what to instrument, why each metric matters to the next release, and what decision each signal should enable. That means owning the six metrics before the first production incident. It means reviewing override frequency alongside completion rate in every sprint review. It means setting the rollback time target before launch, because the target is what forces the recovery workflow to be designed rather than deferred.

The organizations furthest along on this treated the observation phase as a design brief, not a postmortem. Bank of New York Mellon's Eliza platform, running over a hundred digital agents in regulated financial services, designed the observation instruments into the product brief before the first agent was deployed. The checkpoint reviews that caught early drift were a designed function, not a reactive response to an incident.

Launch is not the end of the product lifecycle. It is the moment the observation phase begins.

The last post is: What Keeps an Agent From Becoming a Liability

This post is part of the AI Product Management series on SAP Community. The series is based on "Agentic AI for Product Managers," a lifecycle framework for building, evaluating, and governing agentic systems.

entry.title: AI Product Management: What Keeps an Agent From Becoming a Liability
entry.content:
Post 7 — AI Product Management series

Nine months after launch, a financial controller asked why intercompany transfers were appearing in the exception queue. The investigation revealed that all four drift vectors had been active simultaneously for months. No drift thresholds had been defined at launch. No retirement criteria had been written.

The agent that had passed every launch criterion, been running in production without incident, and generating clean metrics was operating in a state nobody had authorized and nobody had designed a way to detect.

What governance actually is

Most organizations approach AI governance as a committee: a body of people who meet periodically to review the AI program, assess risks, and issue policies. This is necessary but not sufficient. A policy document does not constrain the agent at runtime. It constrains the humans who are supposed to govern the agent. These are different things.

Real governance for agentic systems is architecture. It is constraints enforced at the moment of each agent action, before the action is taken, regardless of what the agent was instructed to do and regardless of what context it received. It is thresholds that trigger automatic responses when drift is detected. It is a retirement criteria written before launch that defines precisely when the agent stops operating. It is built into the system, not administered over the system.

The distinction matters most when something goes wrong at scale. A governance committee cannot respond fast enough. The architecture can.

 The four drift vectors

Drift is not one thing. An agent can drift in four independent directions simultaneously, and the combination can be more dangerous than any single vector in isolation. Check all four at every scheduled post-release review.

Model drift. Has the foundation model version changed since the last behavioral baseline was established? The provider does not reliably notify the product team when this happens. The only reliable way to detect model drift is to run a behavioral comparison against the baseline after each review period. A model update from the provider is a deployment event. Treat it as one: re-run the evaluation suite, compare against baseline, require sign-off before the updated model serves production traffic.

Data drift. Has the input distribution shifted significantly from the distribution the agent was evaluated on? Schema migrations, new customer segments, seasonal patterns, and upstream system changes can move the actual inputs the agent receives away from the inputs it was tested on. A model that performed well on last year's customer service vocabulary may perform worse on this year's vocabulary if product terminology has changed.

Supervision drift. Has the frequency or quality of human review changed since launch? The monthly review meeting that was quietly canceled because "things have been running smoothly" is supervision drift. It does not announce itself. It is discovered when an incident requires a review that has not happened in four months.

Scope drift. Is the agent taking actions outside its originally authorized mandate? This is often invisible until someone asks why the agent is doing something it was never designed to do. It can be introduced by a prompt change that removed an exclusion list, a tool configuration change that added new permissions, or an orchestration change that expanded the agent's decision-making authority.

In the financial postings example from the opening: all four vectors went active over nine months. The model was updated twice with no behavioral baseline comparison. Input patterns shifted significantly when the customer migrated to a new chart of accounts. The weekly review was canceled because "things had been quiet." An exclusion list was silently removed during an unrelated prompt update. No single event triggered a review. The accumulated drift was discovered by a financial controller asking a simple question.

Constitutional runtime rules

Four categories of constraints must be defined for every agentic deployment before engineering starts. These are not policy statements. They are runtime rules enforced at the moment of each action.

Actions the agent may never take. Hard stops. Regardless of instruction, context, or user request. If an action is in this category, the agent refuses it and escalates to a human. This list should be short and unambiguous. If it is long, the authorization model is not scoped correctly.

Actions requiring explicit human authorization. Regardless of confidence level. The agent does not proceed without a named human decision. The agent presents the decision package. The authorization is logged before the action is taken.

Actions the agent may take autonomously. The positively defined scope of autonomous action, with the specific conditions under which that authorization can be suspended or revoked.

The kill-switch procedure. Who can pause the agent, by what mechanism, how fast it takes effect, and what happens to in-flight work when the agent is paused. This procedure must be tested before launch. A kill-switch that exists only in documentation is not a kill-switch.

The authority delegation chain

For every consequential action the agent takes, three questions must be answerable from the audit trail. Not from memory. Not from a policy document. Not by calling the person who was responsible at the time. From the audit trail.

Whose authority was the agent acting under? Not "the system was authorized." Which person or role, in which organizational position, explicitly authorized this class of action?

Which version of the governance policy was in effect at the time of the action? Policies change. The version in effect when the action was taken is the policy against which the action should be assessed.

Who carries the outcome if the action was wrong? If this question has no answer, the system is not governed. It is operating. The answer must be established before the agent is deployed.

If any of the three questions cannot be answered from the audit trail, the system is not supervised. It is merely operating. "The agent did it" is not a defensible answer to any of these questions in any regulated context, and in an increasing number of non-regulated ones.

The Practice Fusion case from 2019 illustrates this precisely. The company paid $145 million to settle federal allegations that it had embedded opioid prescribing suggestions in clinical workflows, paid for by an opioid manufacturer, without disclosure. The prompt worked exactly as designed. The governance failure was not the prompt. It was the complete absence of an authority delegation chain. Nobody had documented whose authority the suggestion was acting under. There was no version control on what was in the suggestion logic at any point. Three fields were blank: whose authority, which policy version, who carries the outcome. This is not a healthcare story. It is a governance architecture story applicable to any enterprise agentic deployment where the agent surfaces recommendations without a traceable chain to a named authorizer under a versioned policy.

Tiered checkpoint protocol

Define the governance response thresholds before the first incident. Three tiers, with specific triggers, written into the go/no-go memo before launch.

Review. One drift vector above its defined threshold. No operational pause required. Scheduled review within the defined window. Document findings. Update the behavioral baseline. Confirm that the governance posture remains appropriate.

Pause. Two or more drift vectors above threshold simultaneously. Or: supervision drift with no replacement oversight defined. Pause new invocations. Review before resuming. When resuming, do not restore the agent to its prior autonomy level without a documented basis for that specific decision.

Retire. Scope drift outside the authorized mandate has been confirmed. Incident recovery time is not improving across three consecutive incidents. One or more of the four suitability conditions from the first post in this series no longer holds. Retirement is a product decision made through a defined process, not an emergency shutdown in response to a crisis.

Retirement as a lifecycle stage

Retirement is the phase almost no team plans for and everyone eventually needs. It is typically triggered by a crisis: the agent produces a serious error, someone escalates, and the response is to shut it down as quickly as possible.

Crisis-driven retirement produces data loss, user confusion, audit gaps, and no institutional learning. Designed retirement has four steps, executed in sequence, with named owners assigned before launch.

Preserve the audit trail before any systems are decommissioned. Archive the complete action trace with the retention policy applied. Once the agent stops running and infrastructure is decommissioned, the audit record may be permanently unrecoverable.

Block new invocations from the moment the retirement decision is made. The agent accepts no new tasks. In-flight tasks are either completed under heightened human review or handed to a human workflow.

Notify affected users with a plain-language explanation of the retirement reason, the date the agent will stop operating, and what the replacement workflow is.

Document the decision: the reason for retirement, who authorized it, which policy version was in effect, and what specific conditions would need to change before a replacement is considered. This is the institutional memory that prevents the same design from being reproposed in six months without the context of why the previous version was retired.

The drift and retirement policy must be written before the agent ships, attached to the go/no-go memo. An agent that ships without retirement criteria defined is an agent nobody has authorized to run indefinitely. But that is exactly what it will do.

What the PM owns

This phase is the most organizational-maturity-intensive phase of the agentic lifecycle. It requires the PM to build structures that involve engineering, legal, compliance, and operations, structures that need to be agreed upon before the first incident.

The practical test: can you produce the four drift vector readings at any point in the product's life? Is the kill-switch procedure written, tested, and assigned to a named owner? Can you answer the three authority delegation questions from the audit trail for any action the agent has taken in the last thirty days? Is a retirement criteria document attached to the go/no-go memo?

If any of those answers is no, the agent is operating. It is not governed.

Governance is not a committee that reviews the AI program. It is a system that makes certain failures impossible, certain decisions traceable, and certain outcomes reversible. It is built before launch, not assembled after the first incident. The teams that do this well do not avoid failures entirely. They contain them.

This post is part of the AI Product Management series on SAP Community. The series is based on "Agentic AI for Product Managers," a lifecycle framework for building, evaluating, and governing agentic systems.

entry.title: Why ERP still defines the limits of AI in the enterprise
entry.content:
Introduction

There’s been a lot of noise around AI and how much it’s changing enterprise systems. It is undeniable that changes are here to stay. We’ve seen teams getting better forecasts, automating things that used to take forever, and catching issues earlier than before. Even we as an enterprise have evolved into something that I have no idea how it would function if AI suddenly disappeared one day.

But after a while, the conversation shifts. You can do countless of things with AI, and I think that is what everyone else is focusing on. And what else? What comes after checking whether AI works or not? What do you actually do once it's there? Getting recommendations is not the hard part anymore. You can have a model suggesting changes, flagging risks, or pointing at opportunities, and most of the time it makes sense. The problem shows up later, when you try to use that in real processes. That’s usually where things start to slow down, and where ERP suddenly matters again.

The Gap Between AI Insights and Execution

We’ve seen this happen more than once. Companies expect faster decisions because they have better insights, but once those decisions need to move through finance, operations, or logistics, everything gets more complicated. Systems don’t always adapt quickly, processes depend on each other, and what looked like a simple adjustment ends up taking way more effort than expected.

In many scenarios, the real challenge emerges when you have to close the execution gap, and not when you have to add more intelligence, as many people may think. It’s something that comes up all the time in conversations with our clients: the need is there, the intent is clear, but the underlying ERP landscape just isn’t ready to move at the same speed. Closing that gap between expectation and reality is where most of the real work is happening today.

The struggle that most organizations have has todo with what comes after they have produce insights. How do you execute a decision through a system that is so structured and interconnected? Is thanks to SAP S/4HANA —and similar ERP platforms— that decision has the opportunity to become reality.

On paper, the issue is simple. AI generates an insight, the business evaluates it, and then we, as organization, execute it. The thing is that, in reality, this chain encounters a vast amount of problems that lead to decisions getting delayed or partially implemented.

In one of the projects we worked on, this became very clear. The company, a global manufacturer in the energy space, was still relying on manual processes to count and track critical components. It wasn’t just slow, it also introduced errors and even safety concerns for the people involved.

By introducing AI-based image recognition, they were able to automate this process and significantly improve both productivity and safety. However, what became clear during the implementation is that the real value didn’t come from the AI model itself, but from how that information was integrated and executed across the operation. The data captured in the field needed to be synchronized with ERP systems, trigger updates in inventory, and align with warehouse and logistics processes in real time. Without that connection, the insight would have remained isolated. It was the ability to translate that intelligence into structured actions within the system that ultimately made the difference.

In another case, a client was using predictive models to anticipate demand changes and adjust procurement accordingly. The insights were accurate, but execution lagged behind. Translating those recommendations into actual purchase orders required multiple validations and manual interventions within the ERP system. By the time the adjustments were fully implemented, the conditions had already shifted. Once again, the limitation was not the quality of the insight, but the ability to execute it in time.

This is why the conversation around ERP is becoming relevant again, especially in the context of AI. It’s no longer just about having a system to run transactions, but about having a system that can absorb change and execute decisions without friction. Concepts like clean core, standardized processes, and flexible extensions are not just technical preferences anymore. They directly impact how fast and how effectively a company can act on what AI is telling them.

Conclusion

What we are seeing, then, is a shift in how these technologies relate to each other. AI is expanding what companies can see and understand about their business, giving them more options and better insights. ERP, on the other hand, continues to define what they can actually do with that information. The gap between those two layers is where many organizations are currently stuck.

The truth is that the companies that will get the most value out of AI are the ones that connect with the models to execution, and not necessarily the ones with the most advanced models. That means treating ERP not as a static backend system, but as a dynamic platform that enables change across the organization. AI might be redefining how companies think, but ERP still defines how they act. In the enterprise, action is where value is created.

entry.title: Exploring AI-Powered SAP Integrations using SAP Integration Suite and LLM APIs
entry.content:
Artificial Intelligence is rapidly becoming part of modern enterprise architectures. As SAP Integration professionals, one interesting area to explore is how integration platforms can orchestrate AI-enabled workflows.

As part of my recent learning journey, I explored how SAP Integration Suite can integrate with Large Language Models (LLMs) and built a simple AI-powered Error Resolver demo using SAP Cloud Integration and Groq APIs.

Understanding the Basics

Before implementing the solution, it is important to understand a few key concepts.

What are LLMs?

Large Language Models (LLMs) are AI models trained on large amounts of textual data. Examples include GPT models, Llama models, Gemini, and Claude.

These models can:

  • Understand prompts
  • Generate responses
  • Summarize information
  • And assist with troubleshooting and analysis

What is RAG?

RAG stands for Retrieval Augmented Generation.

In a RAG architecture:

  1. Relevant context is retrieved
  2. The context is added to the prompt
  3. The LLM generates a more contextual and intelligent response

This improves the quality and relevance of AI-generated answers.

What are Embeddings and Vector Databases?

Embeddings are numerical vector representations of text.

These embeddings are usually stored in Vector Databases such as:

  • SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine
  • Pinecone
  • Weaviate
  • And others

The Vector Database helps retrieve semantically similar information during AI processing.

For this demo, I intentionally simplified the retrieval process using Groovy-based logic instead of implementing a real Vector Database. This allowed me to focus first on orchestration and AI integration concepts.

Integration as an Orchestration Layer

One of the most interesting learnings from this exercise was understanding the role of SAP Integration Suite as an orchestration layer.

Even though:

  • The LLM runs externally
  • Embeddings may be generated externally
  • Retrieval may happen through a Vector Database

The orchestration still remains controlled by SAP Cloud Integration.

The integration flow can manage:

  • Prompt preparation
  • API communication
  • Contextual augmentation
  • Response parsing
  • Final response handling

This makes SAP Integration Suite highly relevant in future AI-enabled enterprise architectures.

Demo Use Case – AI-Powered Error Resolver

The demo use case was intentionally kept simple.

Input Example:
“Mapping failed: InvoiceID missing”

The iFlow:

  1. Receives the error
  2. Builds contextual information
  3. Sends the prompt to a Groq-hosted LLM model
  4. Receives an AI-generated response
  5. Returns the probable root cause and resolution

Technologies Used

The demo was implemented using:

  • SAP Integration Suite
  • SAP Cloud Integration (SAP CI)
  • Groovy Scripts
  • Groq APIs
  • Llama 3.3 70B model
  • POSTMAN for API validation

High-Level Architecture

SAP CI → Prompt Orchestration → Groq LLM API → AI Response Parsing → Final Resolution

At this stage, retrieval was simulated through Groovy logic instead of a real Vector Database.

Key Learnings from this Exercise

Some important learnings from this exploration:

  • AI integrations are fundamentally orchestration-driven.
  • Prompt quality significantly impacts AI responses.
  • Integration platforms can become powerful AI orchestration layers.
  • Understanding APIs and response structures is critical.
  • Starting simple helps in understanding AI architecture practically.

Demo Video

iFlow Design

 

Final Thoughts

This exploration was a great starting point in understanding how SAP Integration landscapes may evolve alongside Generative AI technologies.

There is still much more to learn about:

  • Embeddings
  • Vector databases
  • AI governance
  • SAP AI Core
  • Production-grade RAG architectures.

However, building small practical demos is an excellent way to understand how these technologies fit together in real enterprise scenarios.

The goal is not to become an overnight AI expert, but to continuously explore how modern AI capabilities can complement enterprise integration architectures.

entry.title: New SAP Data Architecture Learning Journey & SAP Certified Data Architect Certification is now Live
entry.content:
TLDR: The Mastering SAP Data Architecture Advanced Learning Journey is now live on learning.sap and alongside it the New Role Play based SAP Certified Data Architect certification is now available in this learning Journey: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/becoming-a-certified-sap-data-architect

Data Architecture Learning Begins here!

Early in my career, I sat in a room full of architects debating a major platform decision. The conversation covered integration patterns, application landscapes, and total cost of ownership. No one discussed the data. Not the quality of it, not how it would flow, not how it would be governed. We treated data as the responsibility of someone else (master data team and BW team) in a different meeting. Years later, we are seeing AI initiatives slow down because of untrustworthy data, I understand exactly what was missing from that room.

Data architecture has spent years as a supporting discipline important, but rarely the headline. That has changed. The shift to agentic AI has moved data architecture from a back-office concern to a front-line competitive capability.

Agentic AI systems do not simply retrieve information. They reason, plan, and act. They require data that is real-time, contextually grounded, semantically rich, and governed with precision. An agent operating on stale, fragmented, or poorly modelled data does not just produce mediocre outputs it can produce incorrect actions. At enterprise scale, that is a governance and business risk problem, not just a technical one.

The architects who will lead in this environment are those who can hold both conversations at once: the strategic (how data drives business value) and the technical (how to design the platforms, integration patterns, and governance structures that make that value real).  

That is exactly what we have tried to address with the New Data Architecture learning Course (Advanced) and a New Data Architect Certification from SAP

Introducing the Mastering SAP Data Architecture Advanced Course

The Mastering SAP Data Architecture Advanced Learning Journey (course BDCDA2) is now live on learning.sap.com. Some of you might have done ‘Becoming a Data Architect – Foundational Course’ which was launched last year and this new course takes a deeper look at the topics related to Data Architecture.

Across four-unit journey designed for architects who are ready to move beyond foundational concepts and into the architecture decisions, framework synthesis, and platform-specific depth that define effective data leadership in the agentic AI era.

  1.  Unit 1: Architecting Data Strategy for the Agentic AI Era

The journey opens with strategy, because that is where most data initiatives fail first. Unit 1 builds the five foundational pillars of data strategy: Governance, Data literacy, Stewardship, Architecture, and Technology enablement and connects each directly to AI readiness. It examines what agentic AI demands from data pipelines that traditional analytics architectures were not built to provide: real-time streaming, semantic enrichment, and contextual grounding. It then moves to leadership and culture, the organizational factors that determine whether a data strategy survives contact with reality and closes with a framework for measuring data strategy success and analyzing real-world value patterns.

  1. Unit 2: Integrating Frameworks for Data Architecture

Architecture without a framework produces inconsistency. Unit 2 examines the three frameworks that define the professional standard in this field: TOGAF, DAMA-DMBOK, and the SAP Enterprise Architecture Framework and, guides how to synthesize them rather than apply them in isolation. It then applies that synthesis to two of the most significant architectural paradigms in the market today: Data Mesh and Data Fabric.

  1.  Unit 3: Harmonizing SAP Business Data Cloud Within the Open Data Ecosystem

Unit 3 is where strategy and frameworks meet the SAP platform. It begins with BDC Connect and the Delta Sharing protocol the mechanism that enables zero-copy data sharing across ecosystem boundaries and then works through each major integration scenario in the open data ecosystem: Databricks, Snowflake (including all three connectivity options), Microsoft Fabric, and Google Big Query. The unit closes with a dedicated lesson on data federation performance optimization the practical trade-offs between federation, replication, and zero-copy sharing that every architect advising on SAP Business Data Cloud deployments needs to understand cold.

  1. Unit 4: Designing Advanced Data Models and Architecture for SAP Business Data Cloud

The final unit addresses SAP Business Data Cloud as a Business Data Fabric how it implements federation, replication, and zero-copy sharing to connect heterogeneous data sources while preserving business context and semantic coherence. It covers Data Mesh in SAP BDC, including how Datasphere Spaces implement the four Data Mesh principles in practice. It then moves to one of the most consequential topics in enterprise AI architecture: semantic models and knowledge graphs as the foundation for trustworthy AI and RAG-based systems.

 Introducing the SAP Certified Data Architect Certification

Alongside the learning journey, we have launched the SAP Certified Data Architect certification.

The SAP Certified Data Architect is an AI role-play-based certification that assesses how well candidates understand modern data architecture in the context of strategic business success. It covers the five major architectural paradigms: Data Warehouse, Data Lake, Data Mesh, Data Fabric, and Data Lakehouse, alongside the core elements of data maturity: governance frameworks, data security, and business-aligned data strategy. It uses interactive question-and-answer format, assessing the understanding of SAP Data Architecture in the agentic AI era specifically the ability to synthesize and articulate how SAP Business Data Cloud enables governed, semantically coherent data access across hybrid cloud environments.

Note: It is recommended that the candidate completes the 1) Becoming an SAP Data Architect and 2) Mastering SAP Data Architecture courses before attempting the certificate to ensure successful completion.

 Who This Is For

The learning journey and certification are designed for practitioners who are already working in the data and architecture space and want to develop the rigor, depth, and formal recognition to operate at senior level. That includes data architects designing enterprise data platforms, enterprise architects expanding their data architecture competency, analytics and BI leaders responsible for governed data access, and technical decision-makers advising customers on SAP Business Data Cloud strategy.

 If you are starting your data architecture journey, the foundational 'Becoming a Data Architect' learning journey on learning.sap.com is the right starting point. The advanced journey picks up where that one ends.

Getting Started

  1. The Becoming a Certified SAP Data Architect Learning Journey live now for both internal and external learners: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/becoming-a-certified-sap-data-architect
  2. SAP Certified Data Architect certification registration: https://learning.sap.com/certifications/sap-certified-data-architect-sap-business-data-cloud

A Final Thought

In the agentic AI era, the constraint on enterprise intelligence is rarely the algorithm. It is the architecture underneath it the decisions made about how data is structured, governed, connected, and made meaningful. Those decisions require a specific set of skills. This learning journey and certification exist to build them.

The practitioners who invest in that depth now will be the ones designing the data foundations that AI-enabled enterprises run on. That work is not infrastructure. It is strategy.

Happy Learning!

P.S: A Big Thank you to @JaSoN_Luo , Sabrina Brück and @Basabdatta_Sengupta  for making this course and certification Happen!

SAP Business Data Cloud

entry.title: New SAP Data Architecture Learning Journey & SAP Certified Data Architect Certification is now Live
entry.content:
TLDR: The Mastering SAP Data Architecture Advanced Learning Journey is now live on learning.sap and alongside it the New Role Play based SAP Certified Data Architect certification is now available in this learning Journey: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/becoming-a-certified-sap-data-architect
